text: Scott Kelley, 50, a fugitive accused of kidnapping that was featured on CNN's "The Hunt" has been arrested by Federal marshals. He was taken into custody at the Atlanta airport after arriving on a flight from Costa Rica. Kelley had been wanted since 2004, for noncustodial kidnapping and unlawful flight to avoid prosecution. His wife, Genevieve Kelley, who is charged with the same crimes, turned herself in to authorities last year, a few months after the case was featured on CNN's "The Hunt." The couple is accused of kidnapping Genevieve Kelley's daughter, Mary Nunes, when she was 8 years old and her father had full custody rights. They then began a life on the run. The search for the three spanned the United States, Canada and Central and South America. It ended when Scott Kelley and Mary Nunes entered the US Consulate in Costa Rica and requested passports to return to the United States.

He has been wanted since 2004 for noncustodial kidnapping and unlawful flight to avoid prosecution. The couple is accused of kidnapping Genevieve Kelley's daughter and fleeing New Hampshire when she was 8 years old. The search for them spanned multiple countries and ended when they entered the U.S. Consulate in Costa Rica and requested passports. Mary Nunes, now 19, was interviewed by investigators at the Atlanta airport and allowed to continue to her final destination. She plans to testify at her mother's trial. text: Federal marshals have arrested Scott Kelley, a fugitive accused of kidnapping his own daughter, after he returned to the US from Costa Rica. Kelley and his wife, Genevieve, are accused of kidnapping their daughter, Mary, when she was 8 years old, and fleeing the country. They were both featured on CNN's "The Hunt." Genevieve turned herself in last year, and her trial is set to begin next month. Scott Kelley's arrest came after he and Mary entered a US consulate in Costa Rica and asked for passports to return to the US. Authorities were notified, and Kelley was taken into custody at the Atlanta airport. Mary, who is now 19, was interviewed by investigators and then allowed to continue to her final destination. text: The jury in the trial of the Boston Marathon bomber, Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, continues to hear the harrowing stories of those who survived the bombings, as well as from the friends and relatives of those who were killed. The jury must decide whether Tsarnaev, who has been convicted on all charges relating to the bombing, should live or die for his crimes. Testimony has been heard from those who watched their loved ones die, and from those who thought they were about to die themselves. The prosecution is expected to rest its case on Friday, at which point the defence will take over. The defence will most likely present evidence in mitigation, and call on experts to suggest that Tsarnaev was under the influence of his older brother, Tamerlan Tsarnaev, who died in the aftermath of the bombings.

text: The Boston jury is hearing testimonies from survivors and families of those affected by the Boston Marathon bombing, in order to determine whether bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev should receive the death penalty. One survivor, Adrianne Haslet-Davis, recalled the day of the bombing when she and her husband were standing near the finish line. She described the force of the blast and her immediate realization that it was a terrorist attack. Haslet-Davis, a ballroom dancer, later had her left leg amputated below the knee. Her husband was also injured and has since admitted himself to a mental facility at the VA hospital. Another witness, Jinyan Zhao, spoke about her niece, Lingzi Lu, who was a graduate student killed in the bombing. The family buried her in Boston, where she had been studying, and placed personal items in her casket. The brother and stepfather of Sean Collier, the MIT police officer who was shot and killed by the Tsarnaev brothers, also testified. Collier had always wanted to be a police officer and his death has had a lasting impact on his family and the MIT police department. The prosecution is expected to rest on Friday. A recent poll shows that 53% of people believe Tsarnaev should face the death penalty.

text:
Boston Marathon Bombing Survivor Testifies: "I Thought I Was Dead"

On Wednesday, Adrianne Haslet-Davis, a survivor of the Boston Marathon bombing, took the stand and recounted the harrowing experience of being near the finish line when the bombs detonated. She described the force of the explosion, how she thought she was dead, and how her husband, Adam Davis, also suffered injuries. Haslet-Davis, who had just returned from a tour in Afghanistan with the Air Force, lost her left leg as a result of the explosion. She begged for whiskey to alleviate the pain and screamed for help. Meanwhile, the jury continues to hear emotional testimonies from survivors and families of those killed by bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev. The case is ongoing, and the outcome remains uncertain.

Adrianne Haslet-Davis, a ballroom dancer, testified at the trial of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the man accused of carrying out the Boston Marathon bombing in 2013. Haslet-Davis and her husband, Adam Davis, were present at the finish line when the bombs went off, and she suffered injuries to her feet. Haslet-Davis described how she crawled through broken glass and debris to reach the Forum, where she found her husband unconscious and bleeding. She also recounted how Tsarnaev, who was captured days after the bombing, had apologized to her husband during his hospitalization. The prosecution presented security footage of Tsarnaev giving the middle finger during his arraignment, which the defense argued was taken out of context. The jury also heard testimony from the relatives of victims, including Jinyan Zhao, who spoke about her niece Lingzi Lu, a graduate student at Boston University who was killed in the bombing. The prosecution is expected to rest its case on Friday.
